text: We are a multidisciplinary group of scientists, students and engineers working at the intersection of remote sensing, climate science and data science. Learn more about each member by visiting their profile.

title: Dr. Sean M. R. Crowell
text: However, I continue to try and I continue, indefatigably, to reach out. Theres no way I can single-handedly save the world or, perhaps, even make a perceptible difference - but how ashamed I would be to let a day pass without making one more effort. - Isaac Asimov

title: Contact
text:
We welcome inquiries from collaborators, prospective students, and members of the public interested in our work.

Email: sean.m.r.crowell@university.edu

GitHub: seanmrcrowell

Location: Rochester, New York, United States

If you’d like to discuss potential collaborations or learn more about our research, please get in touch via email.

title: Carbon Inversion
text: We use inverse modelling to estimate regional carbon dioxide and methane fluxes by assimilating satellite observations into atmospheric transport models. These flux estimates help evaluate ecosystem processes and support national inventories.

title: Carbon Inversion
text: We use inverse modelling to estimate regional carbon dioxide and methane fluxes by assimilating satellite observations into atmospheric transport models. These flux estimates help evaluate ecosystem processes and support national inventories.

title: Carbon Inversion
text:
Outcomes

A regional inverse model for North America that integrates multiple satellite platforms.
Improved priors for biosphere uptake and fossil‑fuel emissions.
Publications in preparation and shared code repositories.

title: Carbon Inversion
text:
Team

Dr. Sean M. R. Crowell – principal investigator.

title: Methane Plumes
text: This project develops methods to detect and quantify methane point sources using high‑resolution satellite imagery. We combine plume detection algorithms, atmospheric transport models and machine learning to identify emission hotspots from facilities such as landfills, pipelines and oil‑and‑gas infrastructure.

title: Methane Plumes
text: This project develops methods to detect and quantify methane point sources using high‑resolution satellite imagery. We combine plume detection algorithms, atmospheric transport models and machine learning to identify emission hotspots from facilities such as landfills, pipelines and oil‑and‑gas infrastructure.

title: Methane Plumes
text:
Outcomes

A detection pipeline for short‑lived methane enhancements.
Cross‑sensor validation with airborne and ground‑based measurements.
Open‑source code and curated benchmark datasets (coming soon).

title: Methane Plumes
text:
Team

Dr. Sean M. R. Crowell – principal investigator.

title: Research
text: Our research combines satellite remote sensing, atmospheric modelling and data assimilation to study greenhouse gases and climate feedbacks. We develop open methods that allow scientists and policymakers to monitor emissions and evaluate mitigation strategies.

title: Research
text:
Focus areas

Methane plume detection – algorithms for identifying and characterising point sources of methane using high‑resolution satellite imagery.
Carbon inversion – inverse modelling to quantify carbon dioxide and methane fluxes across regions and seasons.
Remote‑sensing retrievals – improving trace‑gas retrievals from satellites and aircraft and validating them with independent measurements.
Open tools – building reproducible workflows and releasing software that supports climate research.

title: Research
text:
Projects

Methane Plumes – detecting methane point sources from satellites.
Carbon Inversion – constraining carbon fluxes using inverse models.

We welcome collaborations with other researchers; please contact us if you’re interested in working together.

title: Dr. Sean M. R. Crowell
text: Dr. Crowell leads the Crowell Lab. His research focuses on advancing satellite remote sensing and inverse modelling techniques to understand greenhouse gas emissions and climate feedbacks. He has contributed to missions including OCO‑2 and MethaneSAT and is passionate about open science.
